title: South Korea as a Current Institutional Proof Point
subtitle: National dictionary publication, current institutional use, and why Korea matters now
summary: A compact evidence brief showing that SignWriting is already present inside a public Korean Sign Language dictionary published by the National Institute of Korean Language.
synopsis: South Korea should not be treated as a vague international use case. The opening of the Korean Sign Language Nuri Dictionary on February 6, 2025 places SignWriting inside a national-language institution, a public dictionary interface, and a corpus-based lexicographic workflow. This brief keeps the claim disciplined: Korea is not the documented empirical center that Brazil is, but it is a current institutional proof point showing that SignWriting can function inside official public language infrastructure.

slides.title: Why Korea Needs Its Own Brief
slides.summary: South Korea matters because it provides a current institutional proof point that SignWriting is already inside official public dictionary work.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for why South Korea deserves its own evidence brief.
bullets: Not only a community example, but a public institutional one
Current and visible rather than purely historical
Strongest when treated as a proof point, not a full national map
slides.speech: South Korea deserves separate treatment because it does a different job from Brazil in the infrastructure argument. Brazil is the strongest documented empirical center. Korea shows something else: that SignWriting is already present inside a public institutional language resource in the present tense. That matters because it gives the infrastructure case a current national-language proof point rather than only classroom history or private experimentation.
slides.notes: Distinguish Korea from Brazil immediately. Korea is not the density brief. It is the current institutional proof brief.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[South Korea Case] --> B[Current Institutional Proof]
  B --> C[Public Dictionary Infrastructure]
  B --> D[Current Strategic Value]
  C --> E[SignWriting Legitimacy]

slides.title: The February 6, 2025 Opening
slides.summary: On February 6, 2025, the National Institute of Korean Language opened the Korean Sign Language Nuri Dictionary with SignWriting visible inside the dictionary system.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the February 6, 2025 dictionary opening.
bullets: National Institute of Korean Language
Korean Sign Language Nuri Dictionary
Public opening on February 6, 2025
SignWriting visible in the dictionary interface
slides.speech: The central event is specific and recent. On February 6, 2025, the National Institute of Korean Language opened the Korean Sign Language Nuri Dictionary. For SignWriting, the significance is straightforward. The writing system is not only discussed in Korea. It appears inside a public dictionary system associated with a national-language institution. That gives the Korea case a sharp institutional relevance.
slides.notes: Use the date clearly. Keep this factual and present-tense.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[National Institute of Korean Language] --> B[February 6, 2025 Opening]
  B --> C[Korean Sign Language Nuri Dictionary]
  C --> D[Public SignWriting Presence]

slides.title: What the Dictionary Contains
slides.summary: The dictionary is described as corpus-based and includes Korean equivalents, examples, sign-form images, and sign-language characters in a public search system.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the contents of the Korean Sign Language Nuri Dictionary.
bullets: Built from Korean Sign Language corpus data
Around 1,000 entries at opening
Korean equivalents, examples, and sign-form pictures
Sign-language characters in the public dictionary record
slides.speech: The Korea case matters not only because a dictionary exists, but because of what kind of dictionary it is described to be. The public launch materials describe a corpus-based Korean Sign Language dictionary that includes meanings, Korean equivalents, examples, sign-form pictures, and sign-language characters, with search by sign form and Korean. That is important because it places SignWriting inside a real lexicographic and interface workflow rather than leaving it at the level of abstract endorsement.
slides.notes: Focus on corpus-based lexicography and public interface features.

slides.title: Why the Institutional Shape Matters
slides.summary: This was not a private experiment. It was a public dictionary launch tied to a national-language institution and described with Deaf participation in the compilation process.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the institutional significance of the Korean dictionary.
bullets: Public language institution, not a private pilot
Deaf participation described in the compilation process
Accessible interface with sign-language video support
Institutional legitimacy in the present tense
slides.speech: The institutional shape matters because it changes how the Korea case should be read. This was not a private software demo or an isolated classroom document. It was a public language resource launched by a national-language institution and described as reflecting Deaf participation in the compilation process. That makes Korea useful as evidence of institutional legitimacy in the present tense, not only as a future possibility.
slides.notes: Keep the focus on institution, public access, and Deaf participation.

slides.title: Why This Matters Strategically
slides.summary: Korea changes the burden of proof by showing SignWriting inside current official dictionary infrastructure, not merely in older archives or informal practice.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the strategic significance of the Korea case.
bullets: Current official use matters more than abstract possibility
A public dictionary is infrastructure, not decoration
The workflow appears to depend on existing SignWriting tools
Institutional partnerships can build from reality
slides.speech: Korea matters strategically because it strengthens a different part of the SignWriting case. It shows current official use inside public dictionary infrastructure. That means the conversation can move away from whether SignWriting could ever be taken seriously by a public institution and toward how such current institutional work should be supported, documented, and connected. It also suggests that existing SignWriting tools and workflows are already doing real institutional work beyond the English-speaking academic frame.
slides.notes: Do not overclaim nationwide adoption. Keep the focus on proof point, workflow, and strategy.

slides.title: What Must Be Said Carefully
slides.summary: The Korea case is strong, but it should not be confused with a full national census of SignWriting use or with the broader empirical role that Brazil plays.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for cautions and limits in the Korea brief.
bullets: Not proof of nationwide everyday writing
Not yet a full map of Korean educational use
Not the same claim as Brazil's empirical density
Already strong enough as a current proof point
slides.speech: The strongest tone here is disciplined confidence. This brief does not claim that all of South Korea now writes Korean Sign Language in SignWriting, and it does not offer a full map of Korean educational or community use. It also should not be confused with the different claim made in the Brazil brief. Korea matters here because it is a current institutional proof point. That claim is narrower, and it is already strong enough to matter.
slides.notes: This slide protects the credibility of the Korea brief.

slides.title: What Should Happen Next
slides.summary: The next task is to document the Korean workflow, support the relationship, and treat Korea as a live institutional partner rather than as a passing example.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for next steps based on the Korea evidence base.
bullets: Document the dictionary workflow and tooling path
Translate and synthesize the Korean materials more accessibly
Support current Korean institutional relationships
Treat Korea as live infrastructure evidence, not just a nice story
slides.speech: The right response to the Korea case is follow-up. The workflow behind the dictionary should be documented more clearly. Korean materials and presentations should be synthesized and translated more accessibly where possible. The relationship with current Korean partners should be supported rather than left informal. And this case should be treated as live infrastructure evidence, because it shows that SignWriting is already doing institutional work now, not only in theory or in the past.
slides.notes: End with action and continuity rather than admiration.
